Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a routine. I'd study when I felt like it or when something was due soon. With 34 hours a week across nine courses, I mostly just bounced between whatever seemed most urgent. I learn better by listening anyway, so I'd often put on lecture recordings while doing other things around my place. It wasn't organized at all, just kind of chaotic survival mode.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place depending on deadlines. Some weeks I'd cram everything into a few days, other weeks I'd barely touch anything until the last minute. I didn't have a set schedule because honestly, I couldn't bring myself to stick to one. The anxiety of having so much to do made it hard to even start, so I'd just wait until the pressure forced me to act.
Q3: Usually I logged in because I knew something was due or because I got a notification. I wasn't one of those people who just checked in regularly out of curiosity. It felt more like an obligation than anything else. Sometimes a classmate from my peer support group would message me asking about something, and that would remind me to actually go look at the LMS.
Q4: Not really, no. The LMS never became a natural part of my day. I'd go through phases where I'd log in more, but it never stuck. The interface just felt tedious and I'd rather listen to recordings I'd already downloaded than navigate through all those menus again. My peer group helped a bit, but even that wasn't enough to make it routine.
Q5: The video lectures were the most useful because I'm an auditory learner and I could actually absorb information that way. I'd play them while doing other things or just lying down with my eyes closed. The discussion boards were okay too because sometimes other students would explain things in ways that made more sense than the professor. Everything else felt like busywork to me.
Q6: I basically ignored most of the reading materials and supplementary PDFs. Reading takes so much effort for me compared to listening, and with limited resources I couldn't always print things out. I'd skim them at best or just rely on what I heard in the lectures. The optional quizzes and extra practice stuff? I almost never touched those unless someone told me they were helpful for exams.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see how it directly connected to passing the course. If it was going to help me on an assignment or exam, I'd do it. But if it seemed like extra fluff or enrichment, I just couldn't motivate myself to care. With everything going on and my anxiety about keeping up, I had to be strategic about where I put my energy.
Q8: Optional activities felt completely ignorable to me. Like, if it's not required, why would I stress myself out more? Required stuff already gave me enough anxiety. I know some people do extras to learn more deeply, but I was just trying to survive and pass. The optional things felt like they were designed for people with more time and energy than me.
Q9: Yeah, definitely. I downloaded a lot of the audio lectures and listened to them on my phone when I wasn't near my laptop. I also had a study buddy from my peer support group and we'd talk through concepts together in person or over calls. None of that showed up in the LMS obviously. I took notes by hand too because typing while listening doesn't work for me.
Q10: There were plenty of times I was studying but the LMS wouldn't know. Like when I'd replay downloaded lectures multiple times, or when my peer group and I would meet up to discuss confusing topics. Sometimes I'd spend hours trying to understand something from my notes without ever logging in. The LMS only saw me when I submitted things or clicked around online.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a lecture, watch it once online to get it marked as viewed, then listen to it like three more times offline. Same with any PDFs I actually needed - I'd save them and access them from my phone later. My internet isn't always reliable anyway, so downloading things just made sense. The LMS only captured that initial access.
Q12: There were definitely low points, especially mid-semester when everything piled up at once. My anxiety would spike and I'd just freeze up instead of being productive. Being bored with the material didn't help either - some courses just felt so dry and pointless. When I couldn't see why something mattered, it was nearly impossible to force myself to engage with it.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted or overwhelmed, I'd just do the bare minimum on the LMS. I'd skim through things without really processing them. Confusion was a big one too - if I didn't understand something, I'd just close the tab instead of trying to figure it out. Having limited resources meant I couldn't always access help or extra materials when I needed them.
Q14: Oh yeah, that happened a lot. I'd have a video playing but my mind would be somewhere else entirely. Or I'd scroll through readings without any of it sinking in. It's like my body was going through the motions but my brain had checked out. The boredom made it really hard to actually focus even when I was technically doing the work.
Q15: I procrastinated constantly, especially on things that seemed overwhelming or confusing. My anxiety made it worse - the more important something was, the more I'd avoid it because I was scared of messing it up. I'd tell myself I'd do it later when I felt more ready, but that readiness never really came. I'd end up rushing everything at the last minute.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to actually do stuff. Without a deadline, I'd drift and procrastinate forever. But when one was coming up, I'd suddenly be on the LMS constantly, downloading things, checking requirements, submitting work. It wasn't healthy but it was the only system that worked for me. The panic was a motivator.
Q17: Feedback didn't really change much for me honestly. By the time I got it, I'd already moved on mentally to the next thing. If it was positive, cool, but I wouldn't do anything differently. If it was negative, I'd feel bad for a bit but still not really know how to improve. I needed more immediate, real-time feedback to actually adjust my approach.
Q18: The structure was okay but it didn't really help someone like me who struggles with self-direction. Having nine courses meant I was constantly juggling and nothing got my full attention. The peer support intervention was actually helpful though - having people to check in with gave me some external accountability. Without that, I probably would have drifted even more.
Q19: Things that were clearly laid out with specific deadlines were easier to engage with. But when modules were vague or had too many optional components, I'd get lost and give up. The lack of interactive or audio-focused content made it harder for me since I'm an auditory learner stuck in a text-heavy system. More podcasts or discussion-based activities would have helped.
Q20: I think that could be people who are anxious and checking things repeatedly without actually committing to the work. Or people who are confused and keep looking at materials trying to understand but never getting there. It could also be people who are just going through the motions to look busy without actually producing anything. Anxiety does weird things to productivity.
Q21: That actually sounds like me in some ways. I passed my courses without being super active on the LMS because I did a lot of studying offline and with my peer group. The LMS doesn't see you talking through problems with friends or listening to downloaded lectures for the fifth time. Some people just learn better outside the system and only use it when absolutely necessary.
Q22: A course log can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but it can't show if you actually learned anything. It misses all the offline studying, the conversations with peers, the mental processing that happens away from the screen. It also can't capture your emotional state - whether you were engaged or just going through the motions. It's a very incomplete picture.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and feeling connected to it somehow. It's not just doing the work but wanting to understand it. For me, I rarely felt truly engaged this semester - I was mostly just surviving. Real engagement would mean I'm curious and motivated, not just anxious and trying to pass. I don't think I experienced much of that.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much my peer support group actually helped me stay afloat. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent listening to lectures offline or the anxiety attacks that kept me from starting work. It can't see that I was bored and disengaged emotionally even when I was technically completing tasks. The human side of learning just doesn't translate to data points.
